stories_covered: Figma down to pre-IPO levels as public market multiples compress
DataDog, Palantir, and SaaS peers diverging on growth expectations
Venture model sustainability debate: low-growth vs high-growth sifting
Thinking Machines implosion with co-founders and team departing
Sam Altman vs Elon Musk $100B lawsuit beginning
Replit raises at $9B valuation on dramatically improved product
Lovable at $6.5B as low-code developer platform
Clickhouse raising new round as OLAP winner in AI era
Sequoia investing in both Anthropic and OpenAI simultaneously
harry_takes: Public market compression isn't bad for venture; it's markets correctly sifting high-growth from low-growth, venture wins by staying in the hot trends
Venture fundamentally converts high-revenue multiples into cash via M&A or IPO before they prove cash flow; that's the business model
Thinking Machines is a seed-round failure dressed up as a mega-fund allocation—founder incompatibility and team departures are normal seed-stage risk
Non-technical CEOs cannot recruit top AI talent; you must be a visionary researcher-founder to lead AI-first companies in this era
Sam vs Elon will be constant entertainment and corporate theater, not a meaningful outcome
harry_boldest_quote: Venture is a capital allocation business.
predictions: Jason Lemkin: Replit will hit $700-900M ARR by year-end 2026 on agent-driven improvements; the $9B valuation is justified by product quality jump
Jason Lemkin: Entry-level and mid-career SaaS founders at 50-75M revenue must embrace grind without venture capital or M&A within 12-18 months
Rory O'Driscoll: Thinking Machines insiders can negotiate for company wind-down with 0.8X return, recycle capital into next Anthropic round for 2X upside
Consensus: Clickhouse will capture 30-40B market outcome if OLAP category proves as important as data warehouse was in prior generation
disagreements: Lemkin vs O'Driscoll on old-school SaaS viability: Lemkin says 100M ARR companies with 50-70% growth cannot attract venture capital and must self-fund. O'Driscoll counters that long-term market equilibrium means any stage can work, and describes it as anecdotal jitter, not structural.
harry_role: Harry drives the venture-model-doom narrative and tests it against panelists, pushes back on nostalgia for old multi-stage days, and grounds discussion in brutal capital allocation calculus. He moderates the Thinking Machines debate and emphasizes founder quality as non-negotiable in AI.
notable: Replit's product improvement from unfixable-app failure to building a full startup simulator over holidays illustrates the non-linear jump in AI development velocity, where foundation model improvements compound into order-of-magnitude product leaps that may justify 100X revenue multiples if growth persistence holds.
